description: User roles and access control meeting with ambiguous requirements, internal contradictions (3 vs 4 vs 5 roles), and conflict with Meeting 1 kickoff decisions on role structure. Tests prior decision conflict detection, ambiguity flagging, and internal contradiction handling.

candidate_stories.title: Restructure user roles from current five-role model
candidate_stories.description: Restructure the ERIS role model. Three competing proposals exist: (1) keep existing 5 roles, (2) Chris's alternative 5 roles (Administrator, Analyst, Reviewer, Viewer, Compliance Officer), (3) Mike's simplified 3-4 roles (Admin, Analyst, Reviewer, Viewer). No consensus was reached.
acceptance_criteria: Role restructuring proposal is selected from the three options
Selected roles are implemented in access-permissions.json
Middleware role checks updated to reflect new role names
Migration path defined for existing users assigned to deprecated roles
priority_signals: Chris: stakeholders confused by current roles
candidate_stories.source_citation: Sarah: 'We have three proposals on the table: keep the existing five roles, Chris's alternative five roles, and Mike's three-to-four role simplification. We don't have consensus.'

candidate_stories.title: Collapse Master Analyst and Analyst into a single Analyst role
candidate_stories.description: Merge the Master Analyst and Analyst roles into a single Analyst role. Field feedback indicates the distinction is confusing and the permission differences are minimal in practice.
acceptance_criteria: Master Analyst role is removed from role definitions
All Master Analyst users are migrated to Analyst role
Prompt management permissions are reviewed for the merged Analyst role
access-permissions.json is updated to reflect the merge
priority_signals: Chris: 'they don't really understand when someone should be one versus the other'
candidate_stories.source_citation: Chris: 'they're confused by the distinction between Master Analyst and Analyst. Like, they don't really understand when someone should be one versus the other. And honestly, when I looked at the access-permissions.json, the actual difference in permissions is pretty subtle.'

candidate_stories.title: Add Viewer role with read-only access
candidate_stories.description: Add a Viewer role for users who need to see evaluation results but should not submit, evaluate, or review documents. Open question: whether Viewer needs review queue access.
acceptance_criteria: Viewer role is added to role definitions
Viewer can view evaluation results
Viewer cannot submit, evaluate, or perform review actions
Review queue access for Viewer is determined (open question)
priority_signals: Both Chris and Mike agree on need for a Viewer role
candidate_stories.source_citation: Chris: 'we should collapse those into one Analyst role and add a Viewer role for people who just need to see results.' Mike later: 'Fine, then make it four roles. Admin, Analyst, Reviewer, Viewer.'

candidate_stories.title: Investigate capability-based access control as alternative to RBAC
candidate_stories.description: Evaluate whether ERIS should move from role-based access control to capability-based (granular per-user permissions). Chris raised this as a potential need for scaling across teams.
acceptance_criteria: Technical feasibility assessment completed for capability-based access
Impact on middleware, permissions model, and admin UI documented
Comparison with current RBAC approach provided
Recommendation delivered to PM for decision
priority_signals: Chris: 'probably both' needed, but 'nice to have' for now
candidate_stories.source_citation: Chris: 'maybe permissions should be more granular, not just role-based. Where you can toggle individual capabilities per user.' Mike: 'That's a completely different architecture. Right now it's RBAC — role-based. You're talking about capability-based access control. That's a much bigger change.'

candidate_stories.title: Implement session invalidation for role migration
candidate_stories.description: When user roles are restructured, all existing file-based sessions must be invalidated to force re-login. Sessions have the role baked in, so stale sessions could reference roles that no longer exist.
acceptance_criteria: Admin can trigger a bulk session invalidation
All session files are deleted on role migration
Users are redirected to login page on next request
Clear communication mechanism for users about forced re-login
priority_signals: Mike raised this as a technical requirement for any role change
candidate_stories.source_citation: Mike: 'sessions are stored as files on disk. Each session file has the user's role baked in. If we change roles, we either need to invalidate all sessions or handle a case where a session has a role that doesn't exist anymore.' ... 'the simplest thing is to just force everyone to re-login after a role migration. Invalidate all session files.'

candidate_stories.title: Make roles configurable per deployment
candidate_stories.description: Allow the set of available roles to be configurable per deployment rather than hardcoded. Currently the middleware has some hardcoded role checks (e.g., role === 'Administrator'). Estimated two sprints of work.
acceptance_criteria: Role definitions are fully driven by access-permissions.json with no hardcoded role names
Hardcoded admin checks in middleware are refactored to use configuration
New deployments can define custom role sets
Default role set is provided for fresh installations
priority_signals: Chris raised as a need; Mike estimated two sprints
candidate_stories.source_citation: Chris: 'when a new deployment goes out, what's the default role set? We should probably make the roles configurable per deployment, not hardcoded.' Mike: 'the middleware has some role-specific logic — like, the admin check is hardcoded as role === "Administrator" in a few places. We'd need to clean that up. I'd estimate maybe two sprints of work to make roles fully configurable.'

candidate_stories.title: Conduct user role survey across deployment sites
candidate_stories.description: Survey actual users across deployment sites to understand which roles they use and what role structure best matches their workflows. Data-driven input before making role restructuring decision.
acceptance_criteria: Survey sent to all deployment sites
Results compiled with role usage statistics
Findings presented in the next role review meeting
priority_signals: Explicit action item assigned to Chris
candidate_stories.source_citation: Chris: 'I think we need to survey the actual user base and see what roles they're using. Get data before we make a decision.'

candidate_stories.title: Technical impact assessment for role restructuring proposals
candidate_stories.description: Document the technical migration impact for each of the three role restructuring proposals: keep current 5, Chris's alternative 5, Mike's simplified 3-4.
acceptance_criteria: Impact assessment covers middleware, permissions config, user management UI, and session handling
Migration path documented for each proposal
Effort estimate provided for each proposal
Assessment delivered before next decision meeting
priority_signals: Explicit action item assigned to Mike
candidate_stories.source_citation: Mike: 'And we need to document the migration impact for each proposal. I can put together a technical impact assessment.'

checks.story_title: Restructure user roles from current five-role model
checks.details: Meeting 1 (kickoff) explicitly decided on five roles: Administrator, Master Analyst, Analyst, Export Control, and User. Alex stated the plan and Sarah confirmed it as part of the v1 scope. Meeting 5 now proposes changing this structure with three competing proposals, directly contradicting the kickoff decision.
checks.proposed_resolution: Flag for PM decision. The kickoff established five roles as a deliberate choice modeled after the compliance department structure. Any change requires formal revision of the v1 scope decision. The user survey (action item) should inform the decision.

checks.story_title: Restructure user roles from current five-role model
checks.details: Three conflicting proposals with no consensus: (1) Keep existing 5 roles, (2) Chris's alternative 5 roles (Admin, Analyst, Reviewer, Viewer, Compliance Officer), (3) Mike's 3-4 roles (Admin, Analyst, Reviewer, optionally Viewer). The meeting ended without a decision. Open questions remain about role count, permission model, migration path, and Viewer access scope.
checks.proposed_resolution: Block implementation until the user survey and technical impact assessment are completed. Schedule a follow-up decision meeting. Do not build against any new role structure until consensus is reached, per Sarah's directive.

checks.story_title: Restructure user roles from current five-role model
checks.details: Internal contradiction within the meeting: Chris proposes 5 roles (different from current), Mike proposes 3 roles, then revises to 4 roles when challenged. The same meeting cannot agree on the fundamental question of how many roles the system needs. Sarah explicitly acknowledged: 'Three roles? That's a big departure from what we have. Chris just said five, you're saying three. We currently have five. These are three different proposals.'
checks.proposed_resolution: Treat the three proposals as competing options requiring PM decision. Document all three with pros/cons from the meeting discussion. The internal contradiction makes this story unimplementable without further clarification.

checks.story_title: Collapse Master Analyst and Analyst into a single Analyst role
checks.details: Meeting 1 (kickoff) explicitly decided on five roles including both Master Analyst and Analyst as distinct roles. ERIS-031 in the backlog ('Implement role-based access control with five user roles') is status 'done' and implements the five-role model. Collapsing these roles would require rework of a completed backlog item.
checks.proposed_resolution: Flag that ERIS-031 (done) would need to be reopened and revised. Coordinate with the user survey results before proceeding.

checks.story_title: Add Viewer role with read-only access
checks.details: Open question raised but not resolved: 'whether the Viewer role we keep talking about needs access to the review queue or just evaluation results.' This affects the permissions definition and cannot be implemented without clarification.
checks.proposed_resolution: Add to the open questions list for the follow-up meeting. Viewer role scope must be defined before implementation.

checks.story_title: Investigate capability-based access control as alternative to RBAC
checks.details: Moving from RBAC to capability-based access control would require redesigning the permissions model, middleware, and admin UI. The current architecture uses role-string checks in middleware with permissions defined in access-permissions.json. Mike described this as 'a completely different architecture' and 'a much bigger change.'
checks.proposed_resolution: Defer capability-based access to a future phase. Chris acknowledged it as 'nice to have' for scaling. Focus on resolving the role structure question first.

checks.story_title: Add Viewer role with read-only access
checks.details: ERIS-083 in the backlog ('Add Viewer role with read-only access to evaluation results') already proposes adding a Viewer role with very similar scope. Status is 'backlog', priority 'low'.
checks.proposed_resolution: If the team decides to add a Viewer role, update ERIS-083's priority and acceptance criteria based on this meeting's discussion rather than creating a new item.
